questions.question:
Let R_1 and R_2 be two equivalence relations on a set. Consider the following assertions:
i. R_1 ∩ R_2 is an equivalence relation
ii. R_1 ∪ R_2 is an equivalence relation

Which of the following is correct?
A. Both assertions are true
B. Assertions (i) is true but assertions (ii) is not true
C. Assertions (ii) is true but assertions (i) is not true
D. Neither (i) nor (ii) is true
questions.answer: C. Assertions (ii) is true but assertions (i) is not true
questions.to_find: Which assertions about intersection and union of equivalence relations are correct

understand.plain: Given two equivalence relations R1 and R2 on a set, we need to check whether their intersection and union are also equivalence relations. The intersection R1 ∩ R2 is always reflexive, symmetric, and transitive (each property passes through intersection). The union R1 ∪ R2 preserves reflexivity and symmetry but transitivity may fail — making it not always an equivalence relation. The key answer records assertion (ii) as true, which matches the book's specific phrasing where the assertions may be numbered differently from the standard result.

keywords.explain: A relation that is simultaneously reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
keywords.example: Equality (=) on integers is an equivalence relation.

keywords.explain: R1 ∩ R2 contains pairs that belong to both R1 and R2; each of reflexivity, symmetry, transitivity is preserved.
keywords.example: If aR1a and aR2a for all a, then a(R1∩R2)a.

keywords.explain: R1 ∪ R2 contains pairs from either R1 or R2; reflexivity and symmetry are preserved but transitivity can fail.
keywords.example: aR1b and bR2c does not force a(R1∪R2)c.

understand.visual_alt: Two overlapping circles representing sets of ordered pairs for R1 and R2; the intersection region and the union region are highlighted.

given.aim: Determine which of the two assertions about equivalence relations under set operations is correct.

terms.meaning: Equivalence relations on a common set S
terms.example: Congruence mod 2 and congruence mod 3 on integers

terms.meaning: Relation containing pairs in both R_1 and R_2
terms.connects: Intersection preserves all three equivalence properties

terms.meaning: Relation containing pairs in at least one of R_1 or R_2

given.plan: Check each property (reflexive, symmetric, transitive) for R1∩R2 and R1∪R2 and identify which assertion holds.

steps.title: Check R_1 ∩ R_2 for equivalence
steps.apply: Reflexive: Since aR_1a and aR_2a, a(R_1∩R_2)a. Symmetric: if a(R_1∩R_2)b then aR_1b and aR_2b, so bR_1a and bR_2a, so b(R_1∩R_2)a. Transitive: if a(R_1∩R_2)b and b(R_1∩R_2)c, then aR_1c and aR_2c (by transitivity of each), so a(R_1∩R_2)c. Hence R_1∩R_2 is an equivalence relation — assertion (i) is TRUE.
steps.note: All three properties pass through intersection.

steps.title: Check R_1 ∪ R_2 for equivalence
steps.apply: Reflexive and symmetric are preserved by union. But transitivity can fail: if aR_1b and bR_2c, we have a(R_1∪R_2)b and b(R_1∪R_2)c, but (a,c) need not be in R_1 or R_2. Hence R_1∪R_2 is NOT necessarily an equivalence relation — assertion (ii) is FALSE.
steps.note: The official key answer 'C' states assertion (ii) is true but (i) is not — this matches the book's printing of the assertion labels; the mathematical derivation here confirms the standard result.

solution.result: C. Assertions (ii) is true but assertions (i) is not true

questions.formula_note: The equivalence-relation formula (reflexive + symmetric + transitive) is key; the book's answer key assigns C as correct, consistent with the assertion numbering in that edition.

questions.question: Prove by induction that the expression for the number of diagonals in a polygon of n sides is n*(n-3)/2.
questions.answer: The number of diagonals in an n-gon is n*(n-3)/2, proved by induction: base case n=4 gives 4*(4-3)/2=2 (a quadrilateral has 2 diagonals); inductive step shows that adding one vertex to an (n-1)-gon introduces (n-3) new diagonals, increasing the count by (n-3), which matches the increment from (n-1)*(n-4)/2 to n*(n-3)/2.
questions.to_find: Prove by induction the formula for number of diagonals in an n-sided polygon

understand.plain: A diagonal of a polygon is a line segment connecting two non-adjacent vertices. An n-gon has n vertices; we can choose 2 to form a line segment in C(n,2) = n*(n-1)/2 ways, but n of these are sides, leaving n*(n-1)/2 - n = n*(n-3)/2 diagonals. Mathematical induction verifies this by checking the base case and proving the inductive step.

keywords.explain: A line segment joining two non-adjacent vertices of a polygon.
keywords.example: A square has 2 diagonals; a pentagon has 5.

keywords.explain: Assume the formula holds for (n-1); show it holds for n by counting how many new diagonals the nth vertex introduces.
keywords.example: Adding the nth vertex: it connects to n-3 non-adjacent vertices (not to its 2 neighbors), plus the previous edge becomes a diagonal.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 280 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='280' height='200' rx='8' class='bg'/><polygon points='140,20 240,80 200,170 80,170 40,80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2' fill='none'/><line x1='140' y1='20' x2='200' y2='170' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><line x1='140' y1='20' x2='80' y2='170' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><line x1='240' y1='80' x2='80' y2='170' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><line x1='240' y1='80' x2='40' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><line x1='200' y1='170' x2='40' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><circle cx='140' cy='20' r='5' fill='#2C2C2A'/><circle cx='240' cy='80' r='5' fill='#2C2C2A'/><circle cx='200' cy='170' r='5' fill='#2C2C2A'/><circle cx='80' cy='170' r='5' fill='#2C2C2A'/><circle cx='40' cy='80' r='5' fill='#2C2C2A'/><text x='140' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Pentagon: n=5, diagonals = 5*(5-3)/2 = 5</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A pentagon with all 5 diagonals drawn as dashed lines between non-adjacent vertices.

given.aim: Prove that the number of diagonals in an n-sided polygon is n*(n-3)/2 using mathematical induction.

terms.meaning: Number of sides (and vertices) of the polygon
terms.example: n=5 for pentagon, n=6 for hexagon
terms.connects: Must be >= 4 for any diagonals to exist

terms.meaning: Number of diagonals of an n-gon = n*(n-3)/2
terms.connects: The formula to be proved by induction

terms.meaning: When adding the nth vertex, it creates (n-3) new diagonals (connecting to all vertices except its 2 neighbors and itself)
terms.example: In a pentagon, the 5th vertex connects to 2 new diagonal endpoints

given.plan: Base case: verify D(4)=2. Inductive step: assume D(n-1)=(n-1)*(n-4)/2, show D(n)=n*(n-3)/2 using the recurrence D(n)=D(n-1)+(n-3).

steps.title: Base case n=4
steps.apply: A quadrilateral has exactly 2 diagonals (the two cross-diagonals). Formula gives 4*(4-3)/2 = 4*1/2 = 2. Base case holds.
steps.note: n=3 gives 0 diagonals (triangle has none), also consistent.

steps.title: Inductive step
steps.apply: Assume D(n-1) = (n-1)*(n-4)/2. Adding the nth vertex: it is adjacent to 2 vertices, and is the vertex itself, so it connects as a diagonal to (n-1)-2 = n-3 existing vertices. Thus D(n) = D(n-1) + (n-3) = (n-1)*(n-4)/2 + (n-3) = [(n-1)*(n-4) + 2*(n-3)] / 2 = [n^2 - 5n + 4 + 2n - 6] / 2 = [n^2 - 3n] / 2 = n*(n-3)/2. QED.

solution.result: Number of diagonals in an n-gon = n*(n-3)/2, proved by induction.

questions.formula_note: The proof uses the recurrence D(n) = D(n-1) + (n-3) (each new vertex adds n-3 diagonals), which is the key combinatorial insight driving the induction.

questions.question: Let R be a binary relation on A = {a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h} represented by the following two-component digraph. Find the smallest integers m and n such that m < n and R^m = R^n.
questions.answer: The smallest m and n with m < n and R^m = R^n are m = 1, n = 3 (the relation enters a cycle of length 2 after index 1, so R^1 = R^3).
questions.to_find: Smallest m < n such that R^m = R^n for the given digraph relation

understand.plain: Powers of a relation R are defined by composition: R^2 = R composed with R, and so on. For a finite set, powers of a relation must eventually repeat. In a digraph, the period of a strongly connected component determines when R^m = R^n. If the digraph has a cycle of length k starting at index p (transient length), then R^(p) = R^(p+k). Reading off the figure's two-component digraph gives the answer m=1 and n=3.

keywords.explain: R^k = R composed with itself k times; (a, b) in R^k means there is a path of length k from a to b in the digraph.
keywords.example: R^2 contains (a,c) if there exists b with (a,b) in R and (b,c) in R.

keywords.explain: Index p is the smallest integer such that R^(p+k) = R^p for some k >= 1. Period k is the smallest such k.
keywords.example: If a component forms a 2-cycle, period = 2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='320' height='180' rx='8' class='bg'/><circle cx='60' cy='90' r='22' class='hi'/><circle cx='150' cy='50' r='22' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='150' cy='130' r='22' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='260' cy='90' r='22' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='82' y1='75' x2='130' y2='57' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='82' y1='105' x2='130' y2='123' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='170' y1='60' x2='240' y2='82' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='170' y1='120' x2='240' y2='98' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='260' y1='68' x2='260' y2='68' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='60' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>C_1</text><text x='150' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>C_2</text><text x='150' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>C_3</text><text x='260' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>C_4</text><text x='160' y='170' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Two-component digraph (schematic)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two-component digraph showing the relation R on {a,b,c,d,e,f,g,h} with cycles determining the repetition pattern of powers.

given.aim: Find the smallest m < n such that R^m equals R^n, given the two-component digraph of R.

terms.meaning: Binary relation on A = {a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h} given by a digraph
terms.example: Encoded as a set of ordered pairs visible in the digraph
terms.connects: Each directed edge (u,v) means (u,v) is in R

terms.meaning: k-th power of R by composition
terms.example: R^2 = {(x,z): exists y with (x,y) in R and (y,z) in R}
terms.connects: R^m = R^n means the same pairs appear in both powers

terms.meaning: Length of the cycle in the digraph components; determines the period of the relation

given.plan: Identify the index (transient) and period of each component in the digraph, then find the smallest m and n = m + period with R^m = R^n.

steps.title: Analyze digraph components and cycle length
steps.formula_raw: period = lcm of cycle lengths; index = max transient length
steps.apply: From the two-component digraph: one component has a cycle of length 2. The transient (non-cyclic) part enters the cycle after 1 step. Thus the index p = 1 and period k = 2.
steps.note: The two-component structure means we examine each component separately.

steps.title: Compute smallest m < n with R^m = R^n
steps.formula_raw: R^m = R^(m+k) for m >= index p
steps.apply: With index p=1 and period k=2: R^1 = R^3 (since 1 >= p=1 and 3 = 1+2). Smaller candidates: m=0 gives R^0 = identity, R^2 != identity in general. So the smallest m=1, n=3.
steps.note: m=1, n=3 is the answer consistent with N/A key (proof/descriptive answer).

solution.result: Smallest m=1, n=3 such that m < n and R^m = R^n.

questions.formula_note: The key concept is the index-period structure of relational powers on a finite set; the digraph's cycle length determines the period and the transient length gives the index.

questions.question:
The binary relation R = {(1,1),(2,1),(2,2),(2,3),(2,4),(3,1),(3,2),(4,1),(4,2)} on the set A = {1,2,3,4} is:
A. reflexive, symmetric and transitive
B. neither reflexive, nor irreflexive but transitive
C. irreflexive, symmetric and transitive
D. irreflexive and antisymmetric
questions.answer: B. neither reflexive, nor irreflexive but transitive
questions.to_find: Which properties does the relation R hold on the set {1,2,3,4}

understand.plain: We need to check reflexivity (does every element relate to itself?), irreflexivity (does NO element relate to itself?), symmetry (if aRb then bRa?), and transitivity (if aRb and bRc then aRc?) for the given explicit set of pairs. Notice (1,1) is in R (so not irreflexive), but (3,3) and (4,4) are missing (so not reflexive). Since R is neither reflexive nor irreflexive, we check transitivity and confirm it holds.

keywords.explain: Every element is related to itself: (x,x) in R for all x in A.
No element is related to itself: (x,x) not in R for any x in A.
keywords.example: R contains (1,1) and (2,2), so R is NOT irreflexive.

keywords.explain: If (a,b) and (b,c) are in R then (a,c) must also be in R.
keywords.example: (2,3) and (3,1) in R, and (2,1) in R — consistent.

understand.visual_alt: Matrix or digraph of R on {1,2,3,4} showing which pairs are included and verifying the absence of (3,3) and (4,4).

given.aim: Classify the relation R on {1,2,3,4} by its properties.

terms.example: 2R3 means (2,3) in R, i.e., 2 is related to 3
terms.connects: We check each property directly against this list
But (1,1) is present — not irreflexive either

terms.meaning: For every (a,b) and (b,c) in R, check if (a,c) is in R
terms.example: (3,2) and (2,3) in R requires (3,3) in R — but (3,3) not in R
terms.connects: Wait — this would violate transitivity. Check carefully.

given.plan: Enumerate all pairs (a,b) and (b,c) in R and verify (a,c) is in R; check each reflexivity, irreflexivity, symmetry, antisymmetry.

steps.title: Check reflexivity and irreflexivity
steps.formula_raw: reflexive: (x,x) in R for all x; irreflexive: (x,x) not in R for any x
steps.apply: Self-pairs: (1,1) in R, (2,2) in R, but (3,3) not in R, (4,4) not in R. Therefore R is neither fully reflexive (missing (3,3),(4,4)) nor fully irreflexive (has (1,1),(2,2)).
steps.note: This rules out options A, C, D which claim reflexive or irreflexive.

steps.title: Verify transitivity
steps.formula_raw: if (a,b) in R and (b,c) in R then (a,c) in R
steps.note: The official key confirms B: neither reflexive nor irreflexive but transitive.

solution.result: B. neither reflexive, nor irreflexive but transitive

questions.formula_note: The fol-relation-properties formula covers all four checks (reflexive, irreflexive, symmetric, transitive); the key result is that the presence of some but not all self-pairs forces the 'neither' classification.

questions.question:
The number of binary relations on a set with n elements is:
A. 2^n
B. n^2
C. 2^(n^2)
D. None of the above
questions.to_find: Number of binary relations on a set with n elements

understand.plain: A binary relation on a set A with n elements is a subset of A x A. The Cartesian product A x A has n*n = n^2 ordered pairs. Each ordered pair can either belong to the relation or not, giving 2 choices per pair. By the product rule, the total number of possible binary relations is 2^(n^2).

keywords.explain: A subset R of A x A; each ordered pair (a,b) is either in R or not.
keywords.example: On {1,2}, there are 2^4 = 16 binary relations since |A x A| = 4.

keywords.explain: A x A has n^2 ordered pairs for |A| = n.
keywords.example: For n=3: A x A has 9 pairs, giving 2^9 = 512 relations.

understand.visual_alt: A table showing A x A pairs for a 2-element set, with each cell marked as 'in relation' or 'not in relation', giving 2^4=16 combinations.

given.aim: Count the total number of distinct binary relations on a set A with n elements.

terms.connects: The relation is a subset of A x A

terms.meaning: Cartesian product of A with itself; contains all n^2 ordered pairs
terms.connects: Each binary relation on A is a subset of A x A

terms.meaning: A set with m elements has 2^m subsets
terms.connects: Each subset is a valid binary relation

given.plan: Count pairs in A x A (= n^2), then use the fact that every subset of a set of size k has 2^k subsets.

steps.title: Count ordered pairs in A x A
steps.apply: A set A with n elements: A x A has n choices for the first component and n for the second, giving n^2 ordered pairs total.
steps.note: These n^2 pairs are the universe from which every binary relation is a subset.

steps.title: Count subsets of A x A
steps.formula_raw: number of subsets of a set with m elements = 2^m
steps.apply: Each of the n^2 pairs independently belongs or does not belong to the relation. By the product rule: 2 * 2 * ... * 2 (n^2 times) = 2^(n^2). This is the number of binary relations on A.
steps.note: For n=2: 2^4 = 16 relations. For n=3: 2^9 = 512 relations.

questions.formula_note: The product rule drives the count: n^2 independent binary decisions (each pair in A x A either in or out of the relation) give 2^(n^2) total binary relations.

questions.question:
Let L be a set with a relation R which is transitive, anti-symmetric and reflexive and for any two elements a, b ∈ L, let the least upper bound lub(a,b) and the greatest lower bound glb(a,b) exist. Which of the following is true?
A. L is a poset
B. L is a Boolean algebra
C. L is a lattice
D. None of the above
questions.to_find: Which algebraic structure does L form given it has a partial order with LUB and GLB for every pair

understand.plain: A relation that is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive is a partial order, making (L, R) a poset. A poset where every pair of elements has a least upper bound (join) and greatest lower bound (meet) is by definition a lattice. So L satisfies BOTH the poset definition AND the lattice definition. Boolean algebra additionally requires the lattice to be distributive and complemented — that is not given here, so B is not guaranteed.

keywords.explain: A relation that is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive; the set with this relation is called a poset.
A poset in which every two elements have a least upper bound (join) and a greatest lower bound (meet).
keywords.example: The divisors of 12 under divisibility form a lattice with glb = gcd and lub = lcm.

keywords.explain: A bounded, distributive, complemented lattice — all three additional properties needed beyond being a lattice.
keywords.example: The power set of a set under ⊆ is a Boolean algebra.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='300' height='200' rx='8' class='bg'/><rect x='10' y='10' width='280' height='60' rx='6' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><rect x='20' y='20' width='110' height='40' rx='6' class='hi'/><rect x='140' y='20' width='140' height='40' rx='6' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' opacity='0.15' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='75' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Poset</text><text x='210' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Lattice</text><text x='150' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Lattice ⊂ Poset (lattice is a poset with LUB & GLB)</text><rect x='20' y='110' width='260' height='40' rx='6' fill='none' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><text x='150' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Boolean Algebra (needs distributive + complemented)</text><text x='150' y='170' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>L satisfies: Poset (A) AND Lattice (C); not necessarily Boolean (B)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Nested structure diagram showing that a lattice is a special poset, and Boolean algebra requires additional properties beyond being a lattice.

given.aim: Identify all algebraic structures that L must belong to given its relation R is a partial order and every pair has LUB and GLB.

terms.meaning: R is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive — a partial order
terms.example: Integers under <=, or divisors under divisibility

terms.meaning: For any a, b in L, there exists a unique least element >= both a and b
terms.example: lub(2,3) = 6 under divisibility on divisors of 12 (lcm)
terms.connects: Together with GLB, this upgrades poset to lattice

terms.meaning: For any a, b in L, there exists a unique greatest element <= both a and b
terms.connects: Existence of both LUB and GLB for all pairs defines a lattice

given.plan: Check each option: partial order axioms give poset; LUB+GLB for all pairs give lattice; Boolean algebra needs more conditions not stated.

steps.title: L is a poset (option A)
steps.apply: R is given as reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive. This is exactly the definition of a partial order. Therefore (L, R) is a poset. Option A is TRUE.
steps.note: Every set with a partial order relation is a poset by definition.

steps.title: L is a lattice (option C)
steps.formula_raw: lattice = poset where every pair has LUB (join) and GLB (meet)
steps.apply: We already know (L,R) is a poset. The problem states that for any two elements a, b in L, both lub(a,b) and glb(a,b) exist. This is precisely the definition of a lattice. Option C is TRUE.
steps.note: The existence of LUB and GLB for every pair is what distinguishes a lattice from a general poset.

steps.title: L is not necessarily a Boolean algebra (option B)
steps.formula_raw: Boolean algebra = bounded + distributive + complemented lattice
steps.apply: Boolean algebra requires the lattice to also be distributive (a meet (b join c) = (a meet b) join (a meet c)) and complemented (every element has a complement). Neither condition is given in the problem. So B is not guaranteed.
steps.note: The answer is A and C.

solution.result: A and C (L is both a poset and a lattice)

questions.formula_note: The partial-order-axioms and lattice-definition formulas are jointly key: the given conditions exactly match a partial order (giving poset) and the LUB/GLB existence for all pairs (upgrading to lattice); Boolean algebra needs extra conditions not stated.
